ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002809 represents omurice as a class; the meat-added variant is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers omurice as a general category; the meat-added variant is not separately enumerated. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for omurice or its meat variant. None assigned.

main_ingredients: minced or sliced meat (chicken or pork)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of omurice with meat weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of ketchup-seasoned fried rice with meat wrapped in a thin egg omelette.

nutrient_source.title: Omurice (Japanese omelette rice) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), whole egg (FDC ID 748967), chicken meat cooked (FDC ID 171477), tomato ketchup (FDC ID 170772), and butter (FDC ID 173410); weighted by standard recipe proportions.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for omurice with meat exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from a standard recipe: approximately 55% cooked white rice, 20% whole egg omelette, 15% cooked chicken meat, 5% tomato ketchup, 3% onion, 2% butter/oil. Per-100g values are weighted composites from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries. Values are consistent with published Japanese food composition data (Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan, 2020 edition, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology — MEXT) for omurice.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based mixed dishes.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for omurice with meat. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to ketchup-seasoned white rice mixed dishes (GI ~54–57) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The egg omelette and meat components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 68.25) / 100 ≈ 37.5 / 2 ≈ 18.8, using 350 g serving with 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g (68.25 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from ketchup, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Omurice with meat contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1085 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low to moderate glycemic index from white rice base and ketchup-added sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 18.8 per standard serving, omurice with meat poses a low to moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and consider pairing with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from egg, butter, and meat; elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Omurice with meat provides approximately 1.6 g saturated fat and 105 mg cholesterol per 100 g alongside moderate sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and prefer lean meat variants prepared with minimal butter.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Omurice with meat (オムライス ミート入り; 蛋包饭加肉) is a Japanese yoshoku dish consisting of ketchup-seasoned chicken fried rice mixed with small pieces of cooked meat — typically chicken or pork — encased in a thin, softly set egg omelette. The dish is a fusion of Western omelette technique and Japanese rice cookery, originating in early 20th-century Tokyo and Osaka yoshoku restaurants. The rice is stir-fried with diced onion, tomato ketchup, and seasoning before being mounded on a plate and wrapped or draped with a lightly beaten egg omelette cooked in butter. The result is a mildly sweet, savoury, and slightly tangy dish with a soft, yielding egg exterior and a moist, flavourful rice interior. In Singapore, omurice is served at Japanese casual dining restaurants and Korean-Japanese fusion outlets. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 25 g protein, 17 g fat, and 68 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index (55) due to the white rice base and ketchup sugars. Sodium content is notable (~1085 mg per serving) owing to ketchup and seasoning, making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The egg and meat components contribute complete protein, B vitamins, and iron. Clinically, the dish is suitable for occasional consumption within a balanced diet but warrants attention to sodium and glycemic load for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact entry for omurice with meat. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries weighted by standard recipe proportions, cross-referenced against MEXT Japan Standard Tables of Food Composition 2020. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice-based mixed dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the recipe proportions are well-established.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Fish and seafood' to 'Mixed', as omurice with meat is a rice-egg-meat composite dish with no seafood component. 2. Cuisine confirmed as Japanese (yoshoku sub-category); the serving restaurant 'Korean Cuisine - Funan Mall' reflects a Korean-Japanese fusion outlet context but does not change the dish's Japanese origin. 3. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) using standard omurice recipe proportions; no exact approved-source entry exists. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on white rice-based mixed dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for omurice with meat was found in approved sources. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (145.0), calcium_mg (22.0), iron_mg (0.8), cholesterol_mg (105.0), vitamin_c_mg (1.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (42.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.12), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.18), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.35), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.6), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (110.0), magnesium_mg (16.0), zinc_mg (0.9), selenium_mcg (12.0), and water_g (62.0) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data; no -1 sentinel values are used as reasonable estimates are available from the approved source. 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain pork or non-halal chicken depending on restaurant; consumers should verify with the specific outlet. 7. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Japanese dish and are left null per multilingual rules (mandatory only for Singaporean dishes).
